mappings.rationaleAI: Mao's development of Maoist ideology emphasizing peasant revolution (A.4.d), the civil war and Communist victory (A.5.a), the Great Leap Forward (A.5.c.i), the Cultural Revolution (A.5.c.iii), and Mao's death and its aftermath (A.5.e).

China's full 20th-century history from the republic's development (A.1), interwar years (A.2), war against Japan (A.3), ideological development (A.4), and emergence of the People's Republic (A.5) are comprehensively covered.
South Korea's Japanese annexation (C.1), division after WWII and the Korean War (C.2.a–b), and post-armistice development through economic growth and democratic transition (C.2.c).

Cold War dynamics cover China's international relations including rapprochement with the US, friction with the Soviet Union (A.5.d), and the Korean War as US and UN intervention against North Korean invasion (C.2.b).

US military aid during the war against Japan (A.3.b), rapprochement and diplomatic relations (A.5.d), support for Nationalist Taiwan (A.6), US occupation and alliance with Japan (B.1.d), and US intervention in Korea (C.2).
The war against Japan (A.3), Japan's alignment with Axis Powers and outbreak of war with the US (B.2.b), and Allied counterattack culminating in atomic bombing and surrender (B.2.c).
The Hong Kong agreement with Great Britain (1984) and restoration of Chinese rule in 1997 are directly covered under China's international relations (A.5.d) and internal affairs (A.5.e).

Japan's early 20th-century constitutional government (B.1), rise and fall of Imperial Japan (B.2), militarist rise and WWII (B.2.a–c), and postwar democratization, economic growth, and international relations (B.2.d).
East Asia as a geocultural region encompassing China, Japan, and Korea, with Confucian cultural traditions and Cold War division, provides broad context for the entire section.
Chinese involvement in World War I, Japanese gains, and the formation of a rival southern government are directly covered (A.1.a).
Beijing as China's political center connects to resistance to Peking during the Cultural Revolution (A.5.c.iii) and the forcible repression of the pro-democracy movement in 1989 (A.5.e).
Marx's theories provide the ideological foundation for the development of Kuomintang and Chinese Communist ideologies, particularly Mao's adaptation of Marxism-Leninism (A.4, A.4.d).
Stalin's Soviet Union as the model for Chinese communism and the Sino-Soviet friction connect to the Chinese challenge to Soviet Communism (A.5.c.iv) and friction with the Soviet Union (A.5.d).
Russia's Soviet-era relations with China, including friction and the Sino-Soviet split, are covered under China's international relations (A.5.d).
Japanese imperialism including the continued presence of foreign interests in China (A.2.b) and Japanese military control and colonial rule over Korea (C.1).
Vietnam's relationship with China including the border war (1979) connects to China's international relations and friction with neighboring states (A.5.d).
Japanese conquests in the western Pacific and Southeast Asia (B.2.c) included the Philippines as part of the Japanese wartime expansion.
Japanese colonial rule over Korea (1910–45) with military control and suppression of independence movements directly covers Korea under Japanese rule (C.1).
